1: <header>A comparative life cycle assessment of anaerobic mono- and co-digestion of livestock manure in Bangladesh </header>
Samira Hossain a Shammi Akter b , Chayan Kumer Saha b , Toufiq Reza Kazi Bayzid Kabir Kawnish Kirtania a,*

<list>a Department of Chemical Engineering, Bangladesh University of Engineering and Technology, Dhaka 1000, Bangladesh 
b Department of Farm Power and Machinery, Bangladesh Agricultural University, Mymensingh 2202, Bangladesh 
C Department of Biomedical and Chemical Engineering and Sciences, Florida Institute of Technology, Melbourne, FL 32901, USA</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Digestate storage Food waste Climate change Developing country Landfilling Attributional life cycle assessment|Proper management of biogenic residues, particularly livestock manure and food waste, is a major challenge for Bangladesh. While mono-digestion has traditionally been used on farms for treating manure, inadequate ener- getic output limits its applicability. Food waste, however, is typically landfilled in current practice. Co-digestion of biowaste emerged as an alternative due to synergistic yield and capacity to handle multiple waste streams. However, its environmental performance is underreported, particularly in developing countries. This study aimed to compare the environmental implications of co-digestion and mono-digestion of livestock manure (poultry and cow manure) with food waste from a life cycle assessment perspective for the regional context of Bangladesh. Two inventory cases were considered, accounting for mechanistically calculated (case M) and experimentally reported synergistic biogas yield (case E). Co-digestion scenarios showed net benefits by reducing three of the five impact categories-considerably reducing climate change (up to 117%), eutrophication potential, and terrestrial ecotoxicity in both cases (54.5 % and 55.7 %, respectively). The highest decrease occurred for climate change by diverting food waste landfilling. However, when synergistic biogas yield was considered, acidification potential and malodor air emissions increased by co-digestion owing to a higher amount of hydrogen sulfide and ammonia in the produced gas, thus entailing significant environmental burdens. The key hotspot in most categories was open storage of digestate, necessitating appropriate post-treatment.
2: Bangladesh is a rapidly developing country in South Asia struggling with proper waste management and valorization. The densely populated country reportedly generated 22.98 million tons of solid waste in 2014 (Aurnob et al., 2022), with a per capita waste generation rate of 0.56-0.7 kg/day. More than 70% of the produced solid waste contains biogenic residues of different origins, such as livestock manure, agri- cultural residues, food waste, etc (Rahman et al., 2018). A major portion of biogenic residue ends up in disposal sites, causing GHG emissions and other environmental implications. Due to the high moisture and low calorific content, direct thermochemical valorization of this type of waste is difficult to achieve (Ahmed et al., 2020; Reißmann et al., 2018; Uddin et al., 2019). Therefore, primary biological treatment technolo- gies like anaerobic digestion have emerged as viable option for reducing waste volume while simultaneously producing a renewable energy
carrier. This technology has gained increased attention in developing countries such as Bangladesh due to its simplicity and low cost (Huque et al., 2017; Rahman et al., 2014).

Anaerobic digestion (AD) of biogenic waste is a long-standing and vital technology that provides waste mitigation and renewable energy generation. The main products of anaerobic digestion are biogas and digestate. The latter may be utilized as a fertilizing substance for soil. The application of AD is predominant in the treatment of single sub- strates, also known as mono-digestion. However, anaerobic mono- digestion is faced with some critical barriers. Several studies report that anaerobic mono-digestion is prone to inadequate organic loading, imbalanced C/N ratio (Karki et al., 2021). These factors lead to the formation of inhibitors and toxic compounds such as NH3 and H2S; resulting in low biogas or methane yield (Astals et al., 2014; Mata- Alvarez et al., 2014). Destabilization due to nutrient imbalance during mono-digestion of certain feedstocks may also impact the quality of

digestate. For instance, a low C/N ratio in the feed increases free ammoniacal nitrogen in digestate, reducing its fertilizer value (Andria- manohiarisoamanana et al., 2018; Karki et al., 2021). Anaerobic co- digestion (ACoD) emerged as an optimal alternative or upgrade for a synergistic effect in producing higher energy density biogas, owing to several reasons such as balanced C/N ratio and optimum organic loading of mixed feed (Zhou et al., 2021). From the perspective of energetic valorization, anaerobic co-digestion may perform better than mono- digestion (Xie et al., 2017). Recognizing the environmental effects of co-digestion over mono-digestion is required prior to implementing co- digestion as a sustainable alternative.

Several research publications have examined the environmental ef- fects of co-digestion versus mono-digestion, with the bulk employing life cycle assessment as a basis for comparison (Edwards et al., 2017; Esteves et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2021; Lijó et al., 2015; Poeschl et al., 2012a, 2012b; Tsapekos et al., 2019; Ugwu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2013). Life cycle assessment is a well-established framework for quantifying and optimizing environmental impact and credits. The type of co-substrate associated with environmental discredits and credits and varying as- sumptions significantly impacts the outcomes (Adghim et al., 2020; Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; Chen et al., 2015; Pellera and Gidarakos, 2017; Ugwu et al., 2022; Woon et al., 2016). De Vries et al. compared anaerobic pig manure mono-digestion to co-digestion scenarios with maize silage and roadside grass as substrates (de Vries et al., 2012). Due to environmental penalties for cultivation and land-use change, co- digestion of manure with an energy crop such as maize silage was re- ported to have higher emissions. Pehme et al. investigated the ecological impact of anaerobic mono- co-digestion of cow dung using culti- vated and wild grass (Pehme and Veromann, 2015). Mono-digestion of cow dung (base case), co-digestion with cultivated reed canary grass, and co-digestion with underutilized semi-natural grassland were stud- ied. In terms of emission reduction, co-digestion with unused grass produced the best results. The author emphasized selecting an appro- priate co-substrate which would result in synergistic yield of biogas while also maximizing environmental performance. Several other studies reached the same conclusions and recommended biogenic resi- dues as a potential co-substrate with manure for the best ecological impacts (Fusi et al., 2016; Meyer-Aurich et al., 2012; Velásquez Piñas et al., 2018).

Although recommended by many researchers, very few the number of studies compared manure mono-digestion and co-digestion with waste residues in the context of developing countries. Regional bias is one of the limitations of the existing studies comparing LCA research comparing mono- and co-digestion. Most of the studies are based on industrialized countries in the European region, with a few being eval- uated in other geographical contexts (Díaz-Vazquez et al., 2020; Ioannou-Ttofa et al., 2021). Regional variations in waste types and biogas plant conditions may have an impact on the overall environ- mental performance. Other logistical issues, such infrastructure and services for waste treatment, vary greatly across developed and devel- oping nations. Due to prevailing factors like feedstock quality due to regional changes, the digestion of manure with an organic residue may reveal different outcomes. Conducting life cycle assessment studies for local conditions thus presents opportunities to understand the system scale view.

Anaerobic digestion is a significant step towards attaining SDG goal for Bangladesh since it allows for the integration of renewable energy sources while also providing waste management benefits (Baeyens and Goffin, 2015). The technology was considered promising for Bangladesh, with potential applications in livestock manure manage- ment. Farm-scale biogas plants are considered a decentralized approach toward energy security. To this date, around 90,000 biogas plants have been installed all over the country (Iqbal et al., 2014). Despite such initiatives, a handful of the installed biogas plants remain operational (Hasan et al., 2022; Iqbal et al., 2014).. Low methane in biogas volumes and difficulties in managing post-digestion slurry remain critical

challenges (Hasan et al., 2022). The current management practices and guidelines in Bangladesh regarding both biogas and digestate differ greatly compared to developed countries. The existing biogas plants lack biogas-cleaning facilities for removing hydrogen sulfides and ammonia, thus making its utilization difficult (Chowdhury et al., 2020; Saha et al., 2022). The highly odorous and corrosive hydrogen sulfide makes overall maintenance and downstream utilization such as electricity generation extremely difficult (Wang et al., 2019). In addition, legislations bar the application of digestate slurry as a fertilizer in Bangladesh, resulting in a major fraction to be disposed or stored in open storage (Bangladesh Standards and Guidelines for Sludge Management, 2015; Saha et al., 2022). At the same time, other types of biogenic residues such as food waste are mostly landfilled, causing significant emissions. Co-digestion may emerge as an option for upgrading the energetic performance of existing biogas plants while handling a higher amount of biogenic waste in the existing decentralized biogas plants.

This study attempts to bridge the gaps identified by undertaking a life cycle assessment comparing mono-digestion of livestock manure (poultry and cow manure) and co-digestion with food waste in the regional context of Bangladesh. To the authors' best knowledge, this is the first environmental assessment of anaerobic digestion systems for managing biogenic residues of Bangladesh from a life cycle perspective. The primary goal of this study is to compare the environmental per- formance of co-digestion to that of conventional digestion to identify potential areas for improvement. Evaluating the effects of an alternative strategy for inventory development is another goal of the study. As life cycle assessment depends on inventory data, the lack or modification may cause varied results. In this study, we calculate the mass flows of biogas and digestate in inventory via theoretical model and experi- mental values to investigate how they affect LCA outcomes.

Evaluating the effects of an alternative strategy for inventory development is another goal of the study. As life cycle assessment depends on inventory data, the lack or modification may cause varied results. In this study, we calculate the mass flows of biogas and digestate in inventory via theoretical model and experi- mental values to investigate how they affect LCA outcomes.
6: Bangladesh is a rapidly developing country in South Asia struggling with proper waste management and valorization. The densely populated country reportedly generated 22.98 million tons of solid waste in 2014 (Aurnob et al., 2022), with a per capita waste generation rate of 0.56-0.7 kg/day. More than 70% of the produced solid waste contains biogenic residues of different origins, such as livestock manure, agri- cultural residues, food waste, etc (Rahman et al., 2018). A major portion of biogenic residue ends up in disposal sites, causing GHG emissions and other environmental implications. Due to the high moisture and low calorific content, direct thermochemical valorization of this type of waste is difficult to achieve (Ahmed et al., 2020; Reißmann et al., 2018; Uddin et al., 2019). Therefore, primary biological treatment technolo- gies like anaerobic digestion have emerged as viable option for reducing waste volume while simultaneously producing a renewable energy
carrier. This technology has gained increased attention in developing countries such as Bangladesh due to its simplicity and low cost (Huque et al., 2017; Rahman et al., 2014).

Anaerobic digestion (AD) of biogenic waste is a long-standing and vital technology that provides waste mitigation and renewable energy generation. The main products of anaerobic digestion are biogas and digestate. The latter may be utilized as a fertilizing substance for soil. The application of AD is predominant in the treatment of single sub- strates, also known as mono-digestion. However, anaerobic mono- digestion is faced with some critical barriers. Several studies report that anaerobic mono-digestion is prone to inadequate organic loading, imbalanced C/N ratio (Karki et al., 2021). These factors lead to the formation of inhibitors and toxic compounds such as NH3 and H2S; resulting in low biogas or methane yield (Astals et al., 2014; Mata- Alvarez et al., 2014). Destabilization due to nutrient imbalance during mono-digestion of certain feedstocks may also impact the quality of

digestate. For instance, a low C/N ratio in the feed increases free ammoniacal nitrogen in digestate, reducing its fertilizer value (Andria- manohiarisoamanana et al., 2018; Karki et al., 2021). Anaerobic co- digestion (ACoD) emerged as an optimal alternative or upgrade for a synergistic effect in producing higher energy density biogas, owing to several reasons such as balanced C/N ratio and optimum organic loading of mixed feed (Zhou et al., 2021). From the perspective of energetic valorization, anaerobic co-digestion may perform better than mono- digestion (Xie et al., 2017). Recognizing the environmental effects of co-digestion over mono-digestion is required prior to implementing co- digestion as a sustainable alternative.

Several research publications have examined the environmental ef- fects of co-digestion versus mono-digestion, with the bulk employing life cycle assessment as a basis for comparison (Edwards et al., 2017; Esteves et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2021; Lijó et al., 2015; Poeschl et al., 2012a, 2012b; Tsapekos et al., 2019; Ugwu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2013). Life cycle assessment is a well-established framework for quantifying and optimizing environmental impact and credits. The type of co-substrate associated with environmental discredits and credits and varying as- sumptions significantly impacts the outcomes (Adghim et al., 2020; Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; Chen et al., 2015; Pellera and Gidarakos, 2017; Ugwu et al., 2022; Woon et al., 2016). De Vries et al. compared anaerobic pig manure mono-digestion to co-digestion scenarios with maize silage and roadside grass as substrates (de Vries et al., 2012). Due to environmental penalties for cultivation and land-use change, co- digestion of manure with an energy crop such as maize silage was re- ported to have higher emissions. Pehme et al. investigated the ecological impact of anaerobic mono- co-digestion of cow dung using culti- vated and wild grass (Pehme and Veromann, 2015). Mono-digestion of cow dung (base case), co-digestion with cultivated reed canary grass, and co-digestion with underutilized semi-natural grassland were stud- ied. In terms of emission reduction, co-digestion with unused grass produced the best results. The author emphasized selecting an appro- priate co-substrate which would result in synergistic yield of biogas while also maximizing environmental performance. Several other studies reached the same conclusions and recommended biogenic resi- dues as a potential co-substrate with manure for the best ecological impacts (Fusi et al., 2016; Meyer-Aurich et al., 2012; Velásquez Piñas et al., 2018).

Although recommended by many researchers, very few the number of studies compared manure mono-digestion and co-digestion with waste residues in the context of developing countries. Regional bias is one of the limitations of the existing studies comparing LCA research comparing mono- and co-digestion. Most of the studies are based on industrialized countries in the European region, with a few being eval- uated in other geographical contexts (Díaz-Vazquez et al., 2020; Ioannou-Ttofa et al., 2021). Regional variations in waste types and biogas plant conditions may have an impact on the overall environ- mental performance. Other logistical issues, such infrastructure and services for waste treatment, vary greatly across developed and devel- oping nations. Due to prevailing factors like feedstock quality due to regional changes, the digestion of manure with an organic residue may reveal different outcomes. Conducting life cycle assessment studies for local conditions thus presents opportunities to understand the system scale view.

Anaerobic digestion is a significant step towards attaining SDG goal for Bangladesh since it allows for the integration of renewable energy sources while also providing waste management benefits (Baeyens and Goffin, 2015). The technology was considered promising for Bangladesh, with potential applications in livestock manure manage- ment. Farm-scale biogas plants are considered a decentralized approach toward energy security. To this date, around 90,000 biogas plants have been installed all over the country (Iqbal et al., 2014). Despite such initiatives, a handful of the installed biogas plants remain operational (Hasan et al., 2022; Iqbal et al., 2014).. Low methane in biogas volumes and difficulties in managing post-digestion slurry remain critical

challenges (Hasan et al., 2022). The current management practices and guidelines in Bangladesh regarding both biogas and digestate differ greatly compared to developed countries. The existing biogas plants lack biogas-cleaning facilities for removing hydrogen sulfides and ammonia, thus making its utilization difficult (Chowdhury et al., 2020; Saha et al., 2022). The highly odorous and corrosive hydrogen sulfide makes overall maintenance and downstream utilization such as electricity generation extremely difficult (Wang et al., 2019). In addition, legislations bar the application of digestate slurry as a fertilizer in Bangladesh, resulting in a major fraction to be disposed or stored in open storage (Bangladesh Standards and Guidelines for Sludge Management, 2015; Saha et al., 2022). At the same time, other types of biogenic residues such as food waste are mostly landfilled, causing significant emissions. Co-digestion may emerge as an option for upgrading the energetic performance of existing biogas plants while handling a higher amount of biogenic waste in the existing decentralized biogas plants.

This study attempts to bridge the gaps identified by undertaking a life cycle assessment comparing mono-digestion of livestock manure (poultry and cow manure) and co-digestion with food waste in the regional context of Bangladesh. To the authors' best knowledge, this is the first environmental assessment of anaerobic digestion systems for managing biogenic residues of Bangladesh from a life cycle perspective. The primary goal of this study is to compare the environmental per- formance of co-digestion to that of conventional digestion to identify potential areas for improvement. Evaluating the effects of an alternative strategy for inventory development is another goal of the study. As life cycle assessment depends on inventory data, the lack or modification may cause varied results. In this study, we calculate the mass flows of biogas and digestate in inventory via theoretical model and experi- mental values to investigate how they affect LCA outcomes.

10: Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a system-scale modeling is a system- scale modeling technique for assessing environmental consequences associated with each step of a product or process' life cycle. The general framework of LCA is provided by the ISO-14040 standard (International Standard Organization, 2006). It is currently a widely used and defined methodology for evaluating related emissions from various stages of a product or process life cycle within the scope of consideration. In this study, the attributional life cycle strategy has been applied, thus only considering the environmental changes occurring due to the product and ignoring the consequential effects caused by the product.
11: The purpose of the LCA study is to assess the environmental impact of co-digestion compared to conventional mono-digestion of livestock waste for producing bioenergy. The insights drawn from the results are expected to assist in improving the current management system typi- cally observed in developing countries. An attributional approach was selected for this analysis. System Boundary.

The system boundary is based on an existing medium-sized biogas plant situated at Gazipur, Dhaka in Bangladesh which processes poultry waste generated at an adjacent layer farm in a fixed dome digester. The system boundary and mass flow for this analysis are illustrated in Fig. 1. The following description of the system is common to each scenario considered. The study considers a cradle-to-gate system, where livestock waste available at the farm is utilized as the main input substrate for the plant. The system culminates in the production of biogas being available for energetic use and digester sludge (also known as digestate) is stored in open caverns. A major portion of digestate is stored in open storage systems such as caverns for an indefinite period, after which it is disposed of. Due to legislation against the usage of liquid sludge as fertilizers, digestate in Bangladesh is not commercialized properly as a
Producible biogas Biogas potential (L/ kg VS)
Fig. 1. System boundary and mass flow for anaerobic digestion system.

product, with only a small percentage sold as organic fertilizer at a minimal selling price. Hence, digestate is considered a waste stream. The temporal scope of the study is the plant lifetime, typically 20 years. The system boundary is illustrated in Fig. 1. Hence, mainly two subsystems are considered- the production of biogas or anaerobic digestion and open storage management of digestate with subsequent disposal.
12: The purpose of the LCA study is to assess the environmental impact of co-digestion compared to conventional mono-digestion of livestock waste for producing bioenergy. The insights drawn from the results are expected to assist in improving the current management system typi- cally observed in developing countries. An attributional approach was selected for this analysis. System Boundary.

14: The primary incentive of this study is to compare the conventional mono-digestion and co-digestion systems. The main function of both cases remains the production of bioenergy in the form of biogas, hence the functional unit of this study is considered as the production of 1 MJ of biogas energy.
15: As aforementioned, the aim of this study is an environmental com- parison of co-digesting livestock waste with a co-substrate, as opposed to conventional mono-digestion. Here, cow and poultry manure are considered the main livestock manure treated for simultaneous pollu- tion control and bioenergy production. For co-digestion scenarios, food waste was chosen as the common co-substrate due to its availability and current lack of management. The biogas plant is located near several sources of food waste, such as nearby fruit and vegetable market; hence transportation has not been considered. Additionally, the study aims to assess the effect of using theoretically calculated and experimental biogas yield data on the LCA outcomes. To encompass the stated goals, we consider two inventory cases-mechanistically modeled (LCI.M in- ventory) and experimentally reported biogas yield (LCI-E inventory), with detailed explanation in the following section. We consider con- ventional mono-digestion systems as base cases for LCI.M and LCLE. Mono-digestion of fresh poultry manure is designated as scenario MB1 for LCI.M inventory case and EB1 for LCI-E inventory case. Similarly, co- digestion of poultry manure and food waste is referred to as MS1 and ES1 while co-digestion of cow manure with food waste is indicated as MS2 and ES2. Table 1 shows the summarized scenarios considered for the life cycle assessment.

In this analysis, the inventory for anaerobic digestion has been calculated in two ways and distinguished; theoretically (LCI.M) and using literature-reported experimental values (LCLE). LCI.M model is based upon Buswell-Boyle equations for estimating the biogas yield and composition, hence considered to represent a mechanistic model for inventory development (Achinas and Euverink, 2016). LCI.E model is constructed by utilizing biogas and methane potential reported in the literature, thus repre- senting a literature-informed inventory model, with a detailed descrip- tion of its construction discussed in the Supplementary Material. The feedstock data containing solids content, specific biogas, methane po- tential, and trace element content has been taken mainly from literature data (Table 2). 20: Food waste utilized during co-digestion is assumed to be diverted from being landfilled, and hence the GHG emission of methane for disposal of food waste has been avoided, resulting in offsetting climate change impacts of co-digestion scenarios. The avoided emission of CH4 due to food waste landfilling is calculated using IPCC correlations developed for municipal solid waste disposal. (Eggleston et al., 2006). As country-specific data for landfill emissions is unavailable for Bangladesh, Tier 1 method or default method has been used for calcu- lations. For calculating the methane emission, default values have been taken. For instance, the fraction of degradable carbon emitted as methane (DOCf) was taken as 0.77 and the methane correction factor

(MCF), which considers the type of solid waste disposal facilities, was assumed to be 0.6 as advised for uncategorized landfills. The total degradable carbon (DOC) has been calculated from the given total solids and carbon content of the food waste in literature. As there is no system for landfill gas capture in Bangladesh, the methane recovery (R) and oxidation factor (OX) have been assumed to be zero, indicating that all landfill gas generated is released to atmosphere. The detailed calcula- tions have been reported in Section 4 of Supplementary Material (Table A7, A8, A9).

22: <header>2.2.2. Construction of baseline of the study with foreground data </header>

Data for constructing the full inventory for the scope of the study has been taken from various sources. Firstly, the case study is framed around the data collection from the farm-scale biogas plant at Gazipur. The handling of feed and the management of digestate is considered an overall representation of medium-sized livestock farm-based biogas plants. The operational data such as daily organic loading, preparation of feed, amount of biogas production, plant lifetime, digester types, and design and maintenance have been collected from the site. Typical data on feed capacity and digester sizes for farm-scale biogas plants have also been collected from Rural Development Academy (RDA), Bogra, Bangladesh. The collected information, assumptions, and mass calcula- tions for biogas and digestate production for LCI.M and LCI.E can be found in Supplementary Material.
23: The inventory was prepared using spreadsheeting software, MS Excel, and the impact modeling was done with OpenLCA 1.10.3. The selection of impact methods was based on the primary sources of emissions observed in inventory analysis. Life cycle impact assessments were computed using CML 2002 baseline method for five impact cate- gories: climate change potential, acidification potential, eutrophication potential, terrestrial ecotoxicity, and malodorous air emissions.

33: Climate change impact accounts for CH4 and CO2 emissions from the system. All co-digestion scenarios combining manure with food waste result in a net decrease in climate change (Fig. 2a and 2b). According to the LCA results, when comparing mono-digestion to co-digestion of poultry manure (MS1) and cow manure (MS2), the climate change impact was reduced by 50.4% and 61% respectively for LCI.M. For in- ventory generated using experimental values (LCLE) reported in the

literature, the reductions were 117% and 67.6% for scenarios ES1 and ES2 respectively, demonstrating similar trends. The overall better per- formance of co-digestion scenarios can be attributed to the avoided emissions due to food waste landfilling. Similar trends can be seen in other LCA research comparing mono-digestion VS co-digestion (Ebner et al., 2015; Edwards et al., 2017). Pehme et al. conducted a conse- quential life cycle assessment comparing mono-digestion of cow manure and co-digestion with cultivated and unused grass (Pehme et al., 2017). The author found co-digestion with unused grass yielding 41% better GHG savings mainly by avoiding landfill emissions. Edwards et al. re- ported the same trend for co-digesting sewage sludge with municipal

Fig. 2. LCA impacts for mono-digestion (B1, B2) and co-digestion (S1, S2) scenarios. Climate change impacts for considered scenarios - (a) LCI-M (Mechanistically calculated inventory), and (b) LCLE(Literature-informed inventory; Acidification potential for (c) LCI.M (Mechanistically calculated inventory), and (d) LCLE (Literature-informed inventory); Eutrophication potential for (e) LCI.M (Mechanistically calculated inventory), and (f) LCLE (Literature-informed inventory); Terrestrial ecotoxicity for (g) LCI.M (Mechanistically calculated inventory), and (h) LCLE (Literature-informed inventory); Malodor emissions for (i) LCI.M (Mech- anistically calculated inventory), and (j) LCI.E (Literature-informed inventory).

waste, also considering the carbon sequestration effect during land- filling ((Edwards et al., 2017). Several studies have found that obtaining higher environmental credits for producing methane-rich, high-volume biogas and avoiding the use of fossil fuels leads to greater GHG savings (Edwards et al., 2017; Tong et al., 2019). Hence, avoided emissions and environmental credits have an overwhelming net impact, resulting in co-digestion outperforming mono-digestion in most life cycle assessments.

The contribution of both the biogas-producing unit and the digestate storage to CH4 and CO2 emissions was virtually equal. For the experi- mental and mechanistic inventory models, biogas leakage accounts for 49.9% and 53.5 % of emissions, respectively, whereas digestate open storage accounts for 46.5 % and 50.1 %. This could be traced back to the assumptions made regarding inventory assessment. The percentage of biogas lost due to leakage and the residual digestion of digestate is nearly same (10 % leakage volume and 9.2 % of biogas assumed to be
lost during digestate storage). Thus, the assumptions made during in- ventory valuation have a great impact on the results.

lost during digestate storage). Thus, the assumptions made during in- ventory valuation have a great impact on the results.
35: Climate change impact accounts for CH4 and CO2 emissions from the system. All co-digestion scenarios combining manure with food waste result in a net decrease in climate change (Fig. 2a and 2b). According to the LCA results, when comparing mono-digestion to co-digestion of poultry manure (MS1) and cow manure (MS2), the climate change impact was reduced by 50.4% and 61% respectively for LCI.M. For in- ventory generated using experimental values (LCLE) reported in the

literature, the reductions were 117% and 67.6% for scenarios ES1 and ES2 respectively, demonstrating similar trends. The overall better per- formance of co-digestion scenarios can be attributed to the avoided emissions due to food waste landfilling. Similar trends can be seen in other LCA research comparing mono-digestion VS co-digestion (Ebner et al., 2015; Edwards et al., 2017). Pehme et al. conducted a conse- quential life cycle assessment comparing mono-digestion of cow manure and co-digestion with cultivated and unused grass (Pehme et al., 2017). The author found co-digestion with unused grass yielding 41% better GHG savings mainly by avoiding landfill emissions. Edwards et al. re- ported the same trend for co-digesting sewage sludge with municipal

38: Acidification potential is indicative of equivalent SO2 emissions of H2S, SOx, NOx and other nitrogenous gas as precursors of acid rain. In this study, H2S emission occurs from digester leakage, whereas NH3 emission is caused also caused by both subsystems. Unlike climate change, no environmental credits are attained to offset the acidification potential. According to the LCA results, the performance of co-digestion scenarios varies in trend for two inventory models (Fig. 2c and 2d). Mechanistically calculated biogas amount does not account for the
synergistic or antagonistic effects of co-digestion, only considering the elemental composition of the feed. This caused decreased acidification potential for both co-digestion scenarios MS1 and MS2.

For the LCLE, both co-digestion and mono-digestion scenarios raised the acidification potential. As can be seen from Table 4, the increase in net biogas and consequently H2S and NH3 production during co- digestion is mainly culpable for this. When comparing cow manure co- digestion with food waste (scenario ES2) to poultry manure co- digestion (ES1), the increase was three times higher. Although NH3 emission increased for both co-digestion scenarios, H2S emission varied. This is owing to the different feed characteristics of the manure and higher biogas volume due to co-digestion (Table 2). Poultry manure

contains a high amount of sulfur and nitrogen, compared to cow manure. Because of the diluting effect, co-digestion of poultry manure with food waste (ES1) resulted in decreased H2S emissions. For cow manure, a higher sulfur content can be observed in the waste mix and a 50% increase in biogas volume due to co-digestion. Digesting cow manure with food waste (ES2) resulted in 1.7 times higher H2S emissions than scenario EB2, resulting in a larger rise in acidification potential.

The dual effect of anaerobic co-digestion has been noted by Xie et al, where the author mentioned both positive and negative emissions of minerals can be expected (Xie et al., 2017). Sulfur-rich substrate can give better environmental performance when co-digested with a high carbon feed due to dilution. However, digesting with a high protein waste can lead to an accumulation of nutrients, which may not be environmentally sustainable, despite having energetic benefits. For both scenarios, the uncertainty of the co-substrate properties is imminent when using a highly heterogeneous waste such as food waste. Though the scope of this study ends with biogas production, it is utilized in CHP plants or as cooking fuel. The subsequent valorization steps can further increase AP by increasing SO2 emission, which has a higher factor of emission. Removal of H2S remains the most appropriate solution for downstream improvement for both anaerobic mono and co-digestion of livestock manure.

42: Eutrophication potential refers to the overpotential due to the fertilization of mineral compounds of nitrogen and phosphorus. The ammonia emission from leakage and digestate storage and phosphorous content of the digestate are the main sources of EP in this study. Co- digestion scenarios reduced the eutrophication potential compared with mono-digestion for both inventory cases owing to phosphorus dilution. Lower emissions occurred for LCI.M (33% reduction by MS1 and 7% by MS2) while higher reductions were observed LCLE (54.5% reduction by ES1 and 29.7% by ES2).

The results contradict the previously reported verdict that entails a higher EP for co-digestion. Rodriguez et al. compared the effect of co- digestion of pig manure and different types of waste with mono- digestion (Rodriguez-Verde et al., 2014). The majority of the co- digesting scenarios increased EP, allotting to the higher nutrient

density. Jiang et al. found doubled EP for co-digestion compared to the baseline scenario of mono-digestion (Jiang et al., 2021). Several reasons can be attributed to this, including characteristics of the substrates, as- sumptions made, and avoided emission by considering digestate as fertilizer. Only ammonia is considered a major emission in both studies, whereas phosphorous in digestate is considered a fertilizer rather than an emission.

Few studies report better performance of co-digestion as well by considering avoided emissions of mineral fertilizer usage (Pehme et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2021). Zhang et al. report that applying digestate as organic N fertilizer leads to an increase in aquatic eutrophication (Zhang et al., 2021).

Pehme et al. found a net reduction of EP by considering environ- mental credits of digestate as organic NPK fertilizer (Pehme et al., 2017). Majority of the studies however agree on ammonia being the foremost contributor to eutrophication, with digestate storage being the hotspot. Digestate open storage and land application remain the main contribu- tors to eutrophication potential in this study as well. In this study, both ammonia and phosphorous are considered emissions. Phosphorous, however, has a nearly threefold greater impact than ammonia.

44: Terrestrial ecotoxicity accounts for emissions caused by hazardous heavy metal concentrations in soil. The presence of Zn and Cu in the digestate is the main source of this impact category. The terrestrial ecotoxicity of mono-digestion was reduced in co-digestion scenarios (Fig. 2g and h). Similar to EP, the dilution of the heavy metal contents caused the decrease. For inventory LCI-M, the reductions were 35% and 28% by co-digestion of poultry manure and cow manure respectively compared to mono-digestions. Co-digesting scenarios developed for LCI. E showed a higher decrease because co-digestion requires less digestible solids due to higher biodegradability.
45: Odor emission can cause a major nuisance for anaerobic digestion systems. The ammonia and hydrogen sulfide released from digester leakage and digestate storage are primary causes. Because of the higher sulfur content in the feed, co-digestion scenarios involving cow manure

increased malodorous emissions by 111 % and 70.6% for inventory cases LCI.M and LCI.E, respectively (Fig. 2i and 2j). However, co- digestion of poultry manure with food waste reduced emissions for both inventories (3 % and 33% for LCI.M and LCI.E respectively) due to lower sulfur and nitrogen concentrations in the waste mix.
46: Biogas production and digestate emission had nearly equal contri- butions to climate change and acidification potential. For emissions calculating the effect of nutrients, such as eutrophication potential and terrestrial ecotoxicity, digestate open storage show a major contribu- tion. Digestate open storage thus has the highest environmental impact in this study. Several other LCA findings reach similar conclusions, with digestate management being a critical source of emission for both mono- digestion and co-digestion scenarios. Controlling ammonia emission from digestate has been suggested as a means of decreasing the impact. Employing a closed storage system has been shown to significantly decrease immediate GHG emissions from digestate. The utilization of digestate as fertilizer has been identified as a long-term solution in various studies. However, the fertilization potential of raw and un- treated digestate as compared with mineral fertilizer is debatable, as they are highly odorous and contain various pathogens (Bastabak and Koçar, 2020; Samoraj et al., 2022). Depending on the feedstock and operational parameters, digestate may also contain hazardous heavy metals which may leach if applied to soil(Ai et al., 2020).Moreover, regulations regarding digestate application vary regionally, with many countries prohibiting the application of slurry digestate due to its antagonistic effect on soil. Physical treatments and thermochemical modification of digestate are emerging as alternatives with both ad- vantages and disadvantages. Future studies are necessary to assess the life cycle impact of different digestate management technologies for improving the environmental performances of biogas plants.
47: <header>3.3. Effect of assumptions on the impact assessment </header>

Due to the lack of foreground data, several literature-informed as- sumptions were made for constructing the life cycle inventory for the comparison. One of fundamental basis of LCA remains the availability of data. Thus, understanding the effect of these assumptions on LCIA is necessary for proper interpretation and reproducibility of the results. For instance, the residual biogas potential has been assumed to be 9.2% of the total producible biogas. The emission from open storage of digestate greatly depend on this parameter. While congruent with lab- scale reports, this value depends on the biogas digester condition at a large scale which may significantly differ from controlled conditions in laboratory. Another critical set of assumptions is the distribution of ni- trogen and sulfur in gaseous and solid state due to digestion. Similarly, the distribution of nutrients depends on the feed characteristics, mi- crobial pathway, and operational conditions such as pH, C/N ratio etc. Despite the assumptions, this study contributes to a detailed preliminary assessment of environmental impacts for anaerobic digestion systems in Bangladesh. Future studies can further enhance this work by collecting additional data.
48: Co-digestion of livestock manure with food waste showed net envi- ronmental benefits compared to mono-digestion, decreasing impacts in three (climate change, eutrophication potential and terrestrial ecotox- icity) among five categories. The highest reductions of up to 117% occurring in climate, due to achieving environmental credits for avoiding landfilling of food waste. Hence, one of the potential envi- ronmental benefits of co-digestion in existing on-farm biogas plants occurs by diversion of existing management practices such as landfilling of food waste. For Bangladesh, this presents an opportunity for

decentralized management of food waste via anaerobic co-digestion. Terrestrial ecotoxicity was also lower for co-digesting scenarios owing to dilution of heavy metals (Zn and Cu), with higher reductions occur- ring for inventory case LCI.E.

Acidification potential and malodor however increased, attributed to feed properties and synergistic yield. Upstream decisions such as proper combination of substrates and controlled C/N ratio may have some ef- fects on reducing such emissions. Both anaerobic digestion systems, however, would benefit from downstream treatments such as biogas cleaning processes guaranteed removal of H2S and NH3 emissions. Co- digestion scenarios considering mechanistically calculated biogas yield reduced impacts in all categories, undermining the environmental trade- off of synergistic or antagonistic effects in biogas production. Accurate mechanistic models are thus necessary for better biogas yield pro- jections, which have a significant impact on LCA results.

Open storage of digestate was identified as the key contributor to most emissions. The highest contribution was found for eutrophication potential and terrestrial ecotoxicity, due to being concentrated with nutrients and heavy metals. Digestate valorization for land application is an emerging concept, which may play an important role in an agricul- tural country such as Bangladesh. Future studies should focus on effec- tive digestate management for energy and fertilizing purposes, including environmental consequences.
